coredata.dc:title: Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Wen-Jun
Shan, Dan
Zhu, Rong-Hui
Deng, Sheng-Yuan
Cosnier, Serge
Zhang, Xue-Ji
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, an efficient carbon quantum dots/gold nanoclusters (CQDs/AuNCs) nanohybrid based ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective sensing of cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid (AA) has been established. The blue emissive CQDs, prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment with alanine and histidine, play the role of reference fluorophore. The red emissive AuNCs, capped by 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), serve as a specific recognition element. Different from MUA-capped AuNCs, the proposed CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity to cadmium (II) ions with a detection limit of 32.5 nM, which is attributed to the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cd2+ to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. Unbelievable, in the presence of AA, the above-quenched sensor is recovered gradually. The proposed fluorescence sensor displays an enhanced performance for AA determination in the range from 0.15 to 15 μM (R = 0.995) with a detection limit down to 0.105 μM. More importantly, this fluorescent “on–off–on” method also offers the excellent performance to Cd2+ and AA detection in lake water and in human serum, respectively.

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence “turn-off” assay for sensing Cd2+ ions
Fluorescence “turn-off” assay for sensing Cd2+ ions
Probable mechanism involved in the determination of Cd2+ and AA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21175114 , 21305067 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20130754 ), Ph.D. Fund of MOE for Young Teachers ( 0133219120019 ), Qing Lan project of Jiangsu Province, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 30915015101 , 30920130112012 , 30920140112009 ), and a project founded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Low-resolution (a, b) and high-resolution TEM (c, d) images of the as-synthesized CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid (a), high-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), and Au 4f (d) peaks of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of CQD (a), amino-functionalized CQDs (b), MUA-protected AuNCs (c), and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of amino-functionalized CQDs, MUA-protected AuNCs, and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. (b) Fluorescence spectra of amino-functionalized CQDs, MUA-protected AuNCs, and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. (c) Absorption spectrum of AuNCs (black line) and emission spectrum of CQDs (blue line) under excited at 340 nm. (d) The time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid aqueous solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL emission spectra (λex = 340 nm) of the CQDs/AuNCs ratiometric probe upon addition of different concentrations of Cd2+ (0–250 μM) in HEPES buffer (10 mM) at pH 7.0. Insets show photographs of ratiometric probe containing 0 μM Cd2+ (left) and 250 μM Cd2+ (right) under a hand-held UV lamp. (b) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio of I624/I438 versus the concentration of Cd2+. Inset: linear fitting curve of I624/I438 versus a different concentration of Cd2+. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Selectivity of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid to Cd2+ in the presence of other metal ions (250 μM) in HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.2). The black column and gray column refer to the PL of CQDs/AuNCs in the absence and presence of 250 μM Cd2+, respectively. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

(a) PL emission spectra (λex = 340 nm) of the CQDs/AuNCs containing Cd2+ (250 μM) upon addition of different concentrations of AA (0–200 μM) in HEPES buffer (10 mM) at pH 7.0. Insets show photographs of ratiometric probe containing 0 μM AA (left) and 200 μM AA (right) under a hand-held UV lamp. (b) The relationship between the PL of CQDs/AuNCs/Cd2+ and AA from 0 to 200 μM. Inset: linear fitting curve of I624/I438 versus a different concentration of AA. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Selectivity of the CQDs/AuNCs containing Cd2+ (250 μM) to AA in the presence of other interferential biological molecules (200 μM) in HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.2). The black column and gray column refer to the PL of CQDs/AuNCs/Cd2+ in the absence and presence of 200 μM AA, respectively. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

Schematic illustration the synthesis of dumbbell-shaped CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for “off-on” detection of cadmium (II) ions and AA. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of this work with some methods for Cd2+ detection.
Comparison of this work with other fluorescence probes for AA detection.

head.ce:title: Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sino-French Laboratory of Biomaterials and Bioanalytical Chemistry, School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sino-French Laboratory of Biomaterials and Bioanalytical Chemistry
School of Environmental and Biological Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Grenoble Alpes, CNRS, DCM UMR 5250, F-38000 Grenoble, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, an efficient carbon quantum dots/gold nanoclusters (CQDs/AuNCs) nanohybrid based ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective sensing of cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid (AA) has been established. The blue emissive CQDs, prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment with alanine and histidine, play the role of reference fluorophore. The red emissive AuNCs, capped by 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), serve as a specific recognition element. Different from MUA-capped AuNCs, the proposed CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity to cadmium (II) ions with a detection limit of 32.5 nM, which is attributed to the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cd2+ to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. Unbelievable, in the presence of AA, the above-quenched sensor is recovered gradually. The proposed fluorescence sensor displays an enhanced performance for AA determination in the range from 0.15 to 15 μM (R = 0.995) with a detection limit down to 0.105 μM. More importantly, this fluorescent “on–off–on” method also offers the excellent performance to Cd2+ and AA detection in lake water and in human serum, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The cadmium is an indispensable material in electroplating, metallurgy, dyeing, storage batteries, and semiconductor industries, etc [1]. However, as a non-essential element for human body, uptake of cadmiferous species from the environment over tolerance limits would cause fatal health issues, such as the notorious “itai–itai disease”, and latent prostate, renal or lung cancers [2]. Dawned from historical lessons, analysts now inspect the Cd2+-circulation in anthropogenic activities by chromatography, electrophoresis, colorimetric and electrochemical techniques [3–6]. Especially, the real-time and operando spectrofluorometric monitoring of Cd2+ accomplished its assessment of environmental toxicology [7,8]. Nevertheless, most current fluorescence chemosensors are implemented in a so-called “single irradiation” mode, susceptible to the incident light and other stimulus, that begets false-positive plus less sensitive consequences [9,10].

To evade such problems, ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) methodology was developed based on the self-calibration of intensities from two correlated luminophores [11], and has highlighted improved contrast ratio and marked advantage in recent biomolecule assays and cell imaging. For example, intracellular Cu2+ was visualized through a ratiometric PL tag consisting of a trispyridinyl-diamine-integrated nanohybrid [12], Mn-doped CdSe/ZnS dual-emitting nanocrystals were synthesized to determine organophosphates selectively [13], a ratiometric probe for monitoring pH values inside HeLa cells has been developed by BSA-Ce/AuNCs [14], and a novel core-satellite CdTe/Silica/AuNCs hybrid sphere was used as dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cu2+ [15]. Despite these breakthroughs, the photobleaching of organic pigments as well as low quantum yield (Q.Y.) and heavy-metal components of semiconducting nanomaterials are still pending [16,17]. In that case, prior to luminescent practice, rational design, selection and combination of both reference and reporter to pair an efficient ratiometric couple make the key steps, which probably accompany innovative sensing principles and methods.

In the merits of intrinsic biocompatibility, excellent photostability, and aqueous dispersity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have popularized as an upstart of the nano-carbon family in bioimaging and fluorescent printing [18–20]. However, the preparations of CQDs still involve expensive precursors, intricate processes. While the CQDs obtained always appear broad size distribution, low quantum yields (Q.Y.) and poor biocompatibility [21]. On the other hand, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which typically consist of only several to tens of gold atoms, also have great potential for application in sensor due to their inert reactivity, highly biocompatible and stronger fluorescent intensity [22]. Curiously, whether the preconjugated or preassembled nanohybrid of the CQDs with AuNCs has synergistic effect for biosensing and bioimaging? Herein, inspired by this, for the first time, we developed a new type of dumbbell-shaped CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for “on-off” sensing of Cd2+ (Scheme 1 ). Surprisingly, the already quenched PL could be recovered gradually after addition of l-ascorbic acid (AA). Therefore, the proposed fluorescence sensor also displays an enhanced performance for AA “off-on” determination. Practicably, the proposed strategy managed field tests of Cd2+ in natural water and vitamin C in human serum, demonstrating the superiority of CQDs/AuNCs probe in ratiometric bioanalyses.

Alanine, histidine, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), l-ascorbic acid (AA), aspartic acid, folic acid, vitamin B2, vitamin B6, glucose, glutathione, dopamine, urea, and citric acid were ordered from J&K Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-N′-(2-ethanesulfonic acid) (HEPES), 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxy- sulfosuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. (Shanghai, China). Metallic salts of AgNO3, CaCl2, CdCl2·2.5H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl, NiCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, and ZnCl2 were procured from Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The natural water was sampled from local Xuan-Wu Lake; and the human blood plasma was provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. The detection solution was 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.0). Ultra-pure water was prepared by a Millipore Milli-QTM system and employed throughout the following experiments.

The morphology and size of CQDs, AuNCs, CQDs/AuNCs were analyzed using a TECNAI-12 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Philips) under an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The crystalline structure and the surface functional properties of CQDs/AuNCs were investigated by K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Infrared spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet employing a TENSOR Model 27 FT-IR spectrometer. The fluorescence and the absorption spectra were measured with an FLS920 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments) and a UV-3600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), respectively. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP–AES) was exercised on a J–A1100 (Jarrell–Ash).

The CQDs were prepared by a modified protocol reported previously by us [21]. In a typical one-step synthesis, 2.673 g alanine and 0.466 g histidine were dissolved into 30.0 mL of ultrapure water, respectively. After that, the solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber (50.0 mL) and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. Then, the reactors were cooled to room temperature. The product, which was dark-green and transparent, was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) for 24 h. Finally, the solution was dried at 45 °C by rotary evaporators to obtain solid powders for further characterization and use.

The AuNCs were synthesized through a modified method previous reported by Chang [22]. Briefly, 10 mM MUA (50.0 mL, dissolved in 15 mM NaOH) was mixed with 5 mM HAuCl4 (25.0 mL) under vigorous stirring. Then, an addition NaOH (30 μmol) was added until the mixture became clear. After that, a freshly NaBH4 solution (2 mM, 6.25 mL) was added dropwise and the resulting solution was stirred continuously for 1 days at room temperature. Finally, after dialysis 24 h (1000 MWCO), the purified solution was dried to obtain AuNCs powders for further use.

The CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid was performed via the following two steps: (1) As-prepared CQDs was coated by APTES [15]. 5.0 mg of as-prepared CQDs and 20 μL of ammonia (28 wt%) were added to 1.5 mL of anhydrous alcohol, respectively, and sonicated 15 min to ensure homogeneity. Then, 10 μL of APTES was injected into the solution and sonicated for another 30 min. After that, the mixture was vigorously stirred on a vortex mixer for 3 h at room temperature. Finally, excess reactants were removed by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 20 min) and APTES-coated CQDs were synthesized. (2) The CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid was prepared via an EDC/NHS technique according to a previously described method [23]. Typically, 400 μL of AuNCs aqueous solution (2.0 mg mL−1) was dispersed into 1.5 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0). Then, 100 μL of EDC/NHS aqueous solution (30 mM NHS and 10 mM EDC) was added and the solution was incubated for 15 min under vigorous stirring. After that, 400 μL of APTES-coated CQDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added and the mixture was incubated for another 12 h at room temperature. Finally, excess reactants were removed by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 20 min) and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid was synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn-off” assay for sensing Cd2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cd2+ was performed in HEPES buffer solution. In a typical assay, 200 μL of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid aqueous solution (5.0 mg/mL) was placed in a series of 5.0 mL colorimetric tubes. Then, 3 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) contained different concentrations of Cd2+ ions were added to react with it. Ten minutes later, their fluorescence spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer in the wavelength range 350–672 nm with excitation at 340 nm and maximum emission at 624 nm, respectively. The selectivity for Cd2+ was confirmed by adding other metal ion stock solutions instead of Cd2+ in a similar way. The practical application of the Cd2+ ions determination in lake water was carried out as follow: the water samples were simply filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) prior to use without any other treatment. A 20 μL aliquot of pretreated water samples with different concentrations of Cd2+ were spiked in the CQDs/AuNCs system and reacted for 10 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured.

The detection of AA was performed as follow: 200 μL of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid aqueous solution (5.0 mg/mL) was mixed thoroughly with 3 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) contained Cd2+ (250 μM) for 10 min to quench fluorescence. Then, 100 μL of HEPES (10 mM, pH 7.0) buffer solution contained different concentrations of AA was added to react with it. Twenty minutes later, their recovered fluorescence intensity was acquired, respectively. The selectivity toward the selected biomolecule was determined using the same method as mentioned above. The practical applications of the AA determination in human serum was carried out as follow: the human plasma were subjected to centrifugation (7,000 rpm, 10 min) firstly, after that, a 10 μL aliquot of pretreated serum samples with different concentrations of AA were spiked in the CQDs/AuNCs system and reacted for 10 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured.

The morphology and size of the CQDs, MUA-protected AuNCs, and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid were characterized by TEM. As shown in Figs. S1 and S2 (Supporting Information), both CQDs and AuNCs display a quasi-spherical shape, the average size was about 4 nm and 2 nm for CQDs and AuNCs, respectively. However, the TEM image of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid shows a uniform dumbbell shape with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1 a). It seems the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is assembled by CQDs and AuNCs via a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction. As Fig. 1b illustrated, it can be obviously found that the average size of the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is about 5 nm. The structural details and the atomic lattice fringes could be observed clearly in HR-TEM (Fig. 1c,d). As shows in Fig. 1d, the lattice spacing of 0.30 nm agrees well with the spacing between graphene (002) layers, while the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is attributed to the Au (111) facet [23–25].

The surface functional groups and element states of the CQDs and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid were characterized by XPS. The survey spectrum of the CQDs shows three typical peaks of C1s, O1s, and N1s, with the corresponding content of each element is 59.2%, 21.8% and 18.9% (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). Nevertheless, except for the similar peaks of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, the survey spectrum of the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid exhibits additional peaks of gold and silicon (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, the content of C1s, O1s, N1s, and Au4f is 60.5%, 24.9%, 6.2%, and 5.7%, respectively. The high resolution spectrum of C1s can be divided into four main peaks that correspond to C–C (283.6 eV), C–N (285.0 eV), C–O (286.6 eV), and CN/CO (287.2 eV) functional groups (Fig. 2b), which was similar to the C1s spectrum of CQDs [26,27]. The N1s spectrum reveals two relative nitrogen species of C–N–C (398.8 eV) and N–H (400.2 eV), which have been observed in the case of C1s (Fig. 2c). The Au4f spectrum shows the binding energy of Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 at 86.7 and 82.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d). Notably, the Au 4f7/2 is consisted of two distinct components at 82.6 eV and 83.2 eV, corresponding to Au (0) and Au (I), respectively [23,28]. This observation further indicates that CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid still preserve the same composition and structure as individual CQDs and AuNCs.

Meanwhile, FT-IR spectra was applied to investigate the surface groups of the CQDs (Fig. 3 , curve a), amino-functionalized CQDs (Fig. 3, curve b), MUA-protected AuNCs (Fig. 3, curve c), and CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid (Fig. 3, curve d), respectively. As shown in curve a, a broad peak centered at ∼3500 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of O–H, the low-intensity peaks at ∼1480 and ∼1600 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and the bending vibrations of C–H, whereas the small peaks at 2900 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrational absorption of C–H and bending vibrations of C–O, respectively. These characteristic absorption bands suggest that there are plenty of hydroxyl groups and carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs [25,29]. However, different bands are found for the amino-functionalized CQDs. As observed in curve b, a broad peak centered at 3340 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of O–H and N–H, the peak centered at ∼1080 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 can be easily identified, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations and bending vibrations of C–N [15]. All results suggest that the surfaces of CQDs are surrounded by amino group. As for the case of MUA-protected AuNCs (curve c), there are a stretching vibrations of O–H at 3400 cm−1, C–H stretching vibrational at 2900 cm−1, CO and C–O stretching vibrations at 1630 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1, respectively [22]. The spectrum for CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid (curve d), an additional peak at 1250 cm−1 is exhibited, except for the similar peaks of amino-functionalized CQDs and MUA-protected AuNCs. It further confirms that the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is assembled by CQDs and AuNCs via a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction.

To study the optical properties of the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid, the UV–vis absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra acquired from the aqueous dispersion of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid are illustrated in Fig. 4 . The CQDs have a strong absorption peak at 330 nm (Fig. 4a), which was ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO groups on the surface of CQDs [25,29–31]. A weak absorbance peak at 236 nm was attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [26,28]. As for AuNCs, the maximum absorption peak at 235 nm, a weak absorbance peak at 380 nm can be seen. However, for CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid, except for the similar peaks to those of CQDs and AuNCs, an additional absorption peak can also be observed at 630 nm. In view of this, as shown in Fig. 4b, the optimal excitation wavelengths and emission wavelengths of the CQDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 438 nm) and AuNCs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 672 nm) can be obtained from the fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Moreover, the as-synthesized CQDs show excitation-dependent emission character (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) with high fluorescence quantum yields (40.8%, Fig. S5, Supporting Information). As for CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid, a stronger emission peak at 624 nm from AuNCs and a hump emission at around 438 nm from the CQDs appear together when excited at 340 nm. Furthermore, the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid exhibits higher fluorescence quantum yields (20.02%) than AuNCs (12.77%) (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). Notably, there is a spectral overlap between the emission of CQDs and the absorption of AuNCs (Fig. 4c), which suggests that a slight fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from CQDs to AuNCs might occur. Simultaneously, the photoluminescence decay profile observed for CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is presented in Fig. 4d. From the decay values, the average photoluminescence decay time (τaverage) of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is 9.25 ns. Noticeably, the exciton lifetime of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is decreased significantly to AuNCs (τaverage = 4.16 μs, Fig. S6b, Supporting Information), whereas the lifetime is increased slightly to CQDs (τaverage = 9.15 ns, Fig. S6a, Supporting Information). This indicates that nonradiative recombination processes are dominated in CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid as CQDs [21,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn-off” assay for sensing Cd2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: As expected, different from MUA-capped AuNCs alone (Fig. S7, Supporting Information), Cd2+ is able to influence the fluorescence of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid greatly. Specifically, the addition of Cd2+ (0–250 μM) to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid solution results in rapid and complete fluorescence quenching of AuNCs based on the electrostatic and metal-ligand coordination, while the fluorescence of CQDs almost unchanged (Fig. 5 a). Therefore, a ratiometric strategy with a sensitive response to Cd2+ is established and actually the CQDs work as the reference fluorophore and AuNCs serve as the recognition unit. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the AuNCs and CQDs (I 624/I 438) versus the concentration of Cd2+ was investigated. A linear calibration was plotted over four orders of magnitudes from 4.0 × 10−2 to 1.5 × 101 μM with a quenching efficiency of 9.0 × 104 M−1 and a correlation coefficient of 0.996 (Fig. 5b, inset). The detection limit of 32.5 nM based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 is lower than the maximum concentration (40 nM) of Cd2+ for bottled water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO) standards [1–3]. Furthermore, the performance of the developed method for Cd2+ determination was compared with those recently reported in the literature (Table 1 ) [32–35]. It appears that a comparatively high sensitivity, and low detection limit can be obtained with this developed method.

To verify this “turn−off” selectivity towards Cd2+, the fluorescence responses of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid to 250 μM Cd2+ were compared with 250 μM of other metal ions, namely, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Co2+ and Mn2+. As shown in Fig. 6 , in the presence of other ions, no apparent fluorescence quenching effects of the solutions are observed as Cd2+ (black columns in Fig. 6), However, when we added 250 μM Cd2+ to the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid solution containing other metal ions, the intensity ratio (I 624/I 438) decreased remarkably (gray columns in Fig. 6). It demonstrates that these metal ions have negligible interference on the detection of Cd2+. On the other hand, it indicates the high specificity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor to Cd2+ over other metal ions. To further investigate the potential practical applications of this method, the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid was employed to detect Cd2+ content in lake water with an outcome of 41.9 ± 3.1 nM, approximating that of 36.8 nM attained from ICP. Furthermore, as shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information), Cd2+ concentrations obtained by the proposed method are well in agreement with the values added before, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensing platform.

It has been reported that AA could slowly decompose at 60 °C in the presence of cadmium ions and formed a cadmium oxalate polymeric species with formula Cd(C2O4)⋅3H2O [36]. Therefore, based on a competition mechanism, we also investigated the sensitive response of CQDs/AuNCs/Cd2+ to AA. Fortunately, we found the addition of AA to the quenching sensor of CQDs/AuNCs would lead to a fluorescence recovery or enhancement. It is an attractive protocol because the “turn-on” fluorescence sensor is less common but more sensitivity than the “turn-off”. Specifically, as indicated in Fig. 7 a, upon addition of different concentrations of AA (0–200 μM) to the CQDs/AuNCs sensor containing Cd2+ (250 μM), the quenched sensor of CQDs/AuNCs is recovered generally. As a result, the performance of fluorescence recovery or enhancement versus the concentration of AA was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 7b, the ratio I 624/I 438 of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid gradually increased with the addition of AA. A linear calibration plot in the concentration range of 0.15–15 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.995 is displayed (Fig. 7b, inset), and the detection limit down to 105.1 nM is obtained based on a 3δ/slope. To our knowledge, the linear range and the detection limit are lower than those recently reported by other fluorescent chemosensors of AA detection (Table 2 ) [37–43].

As a necessary operation, the selectivity of Cd2+/CQDs/AuNCs in the ratiometric response to AA was explored against several endogenous substances of glucose, dopamine, urea, glutathione, citric acid, vitamin B2/B6, aspartic acid and folic acid. As clearly shown in Fig. 8 , when 200 μM AA and other foreign substances were added to the CQDs/AuNCs sensor containing Cd2+ (250 μM), only AA displayed a remarkable enhancement, which suggested the good selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor to AA over other substances. Moreover, for further applied in clinical analysis and the pharmaceutical industry, the sensing of AA assay in human serum was carried out. As shown in Table S2 (Supporting Information), AA concentrations obtained by the ratiometric fluorescent sensor are well in agreement with the values added before, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probable mechanism involved in the determination of Cd2+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the fluorescence quenching involves static quenching (formation of a ground-state non-fluorescent complex), dynamic quenching (electron transfer processes) and the inner filter effect (IFE) [40,44]. When 250 μM Cd2+ was added into the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid solution, the initial dispersed nanohybrid could aggregate into large cubic particles (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). However, we found the amino-functionalized CQDs and MUA-capped AuNCs solution had not aggregates at the same condition. Since the specific recognition sites of CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid, which can effectively adsorb the Cd2+ due to electrostatic interactions on one hand and metal-ligand coordination on the other, lead to the formation of cubic aggregates. Owing to the strong quenching ability of cubic aggregates, the fluorescence of AuNCs can be quenched by efficient nonradiative energy transfer. Therefore, such a quenching mechanism is known as static quenching. Moreover, the absorbance of cubic aggregates at both the excitation (λex = 340 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 624 nm) is substantial (Fig. S9a, Supporting Information), which can also cause the fluorescence decrease. It aslo agrees that the lifetime of CQDs/AuNCs/Cd2+ (τaverage = 10.11 ns, Fig. S9b, Supporting Information) is longer than CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. Thus, the contribution of IFE to the observed fluorescence quenching cannot be neglected. However, upon addition of different concentrations of AA (0–200 μM) to the CQDs/AuNCs sensor containing Cd2+ (250 μM), AA could bind with Cd2+ and disaggregate the cubic aggregates. As a result, the solution of CQDs/AuNCs–Cd2+ become transparent and the quenched sensor of CQDs/AuNCs is recovered generally.

In summary, a novel protocol for Cd2+ fluorescence “turn off” and AA fluorescence “turn on” determination was established and its mechanism was discussed. Specifically, this nanohybrid system possesses dual emission peaks at 438 and 624 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 340 nm. The addition of Cd2+ to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid solution leads to the formation of cubic aggregates, results in rapid and complete fluorescence quenching of AuNCs. However, upon addition of AA (0–200 μM) to the nanohybrid system containing Cd2+ (250 μM), AA could bind with Cd2+ and destroy the cubic aggregates. As a result, the solution of CQDs/AuNCs–Cd2+ become transparent and the quenched sensor of CQDs/AuNCs is recovered generally. Moreover, it offers the excellent performance to Cd2+ and AA detection in lake water and in human serum, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.21175114, 21305067), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20130754), Ph.D. Fund of MOE for Young Teachers (0133219120019), Qing Lan project of Jiangsu Province, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30915015101, 30920130112012, 30920140112009), and a project founded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.10.051.
